title: Why Decoders Struggle
concept: The decoder starts generating target text using only the bottleneck vector; as the output grows, the starting context fades, causing mistakes.

exercises.prompt: What is the main source of information the decoder starts with to generate target text?
exercises.explain: The decoder uses a compressed representation, often called the bottleneck vector, to generate the output translation.

cards.title: The Memory Fade
cards.body: Have you ever tried to memorize a long shopping list, only to forget the last few items by the time you reach the back of the store? AI decoders face a similar struggle when trying to write long sentences using only a tiny memory package!

cards.title: Why Decoders Struggle
cards.body: Decoders rely on a single compressed package called a 'bottleneck vector' to start writing. Because this package has a fixed size, it cannot hold all the details of long sentences. As the decoder writes more and more words, the memory of that initial package fades, leading to mistakes.

cards.title: The Tiny Photo Analogy
cards.body: Imagine packing your entire family vacation memory into a single pocket-sized photo. When you first look at the photo, you remember everything clearly. But as you try to write a 10-page story based *only* on that tiny photo, you start forgetting the details of what happened by page 5 because the photo can only hold so much.

cards.title: Short vs. Long Sentences
cards.body: If you translate a short sentence like "I love cats," the decoder works perfectly. But if you translate a paragraph with 50 words, the decoder will start strong but might completely forget the middle details or mess up the ending because the starting bottleneck vector ran out of detail.

cards.title: It's Not a Lack of Brainpower!
cards.body: Some people think decoders struggle because they aren't smart enough or lack training. In reality, the bottleneck structure forces them to fit a mountain of information into a tiny molehill. No matter how smart the AI is, a tight bottleneck limits its memory.

cards.title: Short vs. Long Translation
cards.body: See how the length of the sentence impacts the decoder's ability to keep track of details.

left.text: Small amount of info fits easily in the bottleneck. The decoder translates it perfectly.

right.text: Too much info overflows the bottleneck. The decoder forgets details by the end.

exercises.prompt: What is the main limitation of the bottleneck vector?

options.text: It automatically expands to any size.
It has a fixed size and cannot hold unlimited detail.
It only stores the very last word of the input.
It deletes itself after the first word is translated.

exercises.explain: The bottleneck vector has a fixed size, which means it cannot scale up to hold more information for longer sentences.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Decoders find it easier to translate long sentences than short sentences.
exercises.explain: Decoders struggle more with long sentences because the fixed-size bottleneck vector cannot retain all the necessary details.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to describe why the decoder makes mistakes on long sentences.
exercises.sentence: As the generated text grows longer, the starting context from the bottleneck vector [blank].
exercises.explain: As the output text gets longer, the initial memory from the bottleneck vector fades, causing the AI to make mistakes.

exercises.prompt: Which AI system shows the classic 'decoder struggle' when translating a long story?

options.text: AI Model A: Translates the first half of a text and stops completely.
AI Model B: Translates a long paragraph but loses the original meaning near the end.
AI Model C: Translates everything perfectly regardless of length.

exercises.explain: Standard decoders rely entirely on the initial bottleneck vector, which loses its influence as the output length increases.

exercises.prompt: Why do standard decoders struggle to translate long sentences accurately?

options.text: The decoder forgets because the bottleneck vector gets larger over time.
The decoder struggles because the starting context fades as the generated text grows.
The encoder stops sending data to the decoder halfway through.
The decoder is blocked from using its own previously generated words.

exercises.explain: Decoders fail because they try to generate long sentences from a single, fixed-size bottleneck vector. As the output grows, the influence of this starting vector fades, leading to errors.
